blocks.text: Every webpage — including your Simon Says game — is built with three languages: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

blocks.alt: Three cards showing HTML structures the page, CSS styles it, and JavaScript makes it interactive
blocks.caption: Three languages, three jobs. Each one does only its part in the Simon-Says game.

blocks.text: Click the buttons below to see how each language contributes to the game.

blocks.label: HOW THEY CONNECT
blocks.text: **HTML** puts things on the page. **CSS** makes them look right. **JavaScript** makes them do things.

content.heading: The DOM — JavaScript's way of grabbing HTML

blocks.text: The DOM is the bridge between your HTML and your JavaScript. HTML builds the elements on the page. JavaScript uses the DOM to find them and change them.

blocks.alt: Three boxes showing HTML building an element, the DOM finding it, and JavaScript changing it
blocks.caption: HTML builds it. The DOM finds it. JavaScript changes it.

blocks.desc: Find the HTML element whose id is 'score'. Store it in a variable so you can use it.
Change what's written inside the element. The page updates instantly.

blocks.label: IDs must match exactly
blocks.text: The id you pass to getElementById must match the id in your HTML exactly — same spelling, same capitalisation. One wrong character and JavaScript returns null.

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: When a square lights up in the game, JavaScript found that button using getElementById and added a class to it. That's the whole trick.

exercises.title: Find and change
tasks:
The page has a

element with id='message'. Find it using getElementById.
Change its textContent to 'JavaScript changed this!'.
Log the element to the console — what do you see?
Now change the text a second time to something else. Does the first change disappear?
exercises.hint: Store the element in a variable first: let el = document.getElementById('message'). Then use el.textContent = '...'

checks.label: the message element's text was changed

exercises.title: Light up a square
tasks: The page has a <div> with id='square'. Find it.
Add the class 'lit' to it using classList.add.
Check the page — the square should turn yellow.
Now remove the class using classList.remove('lit'). What happens?
Add it back. This is exactly what Simon Says does on every turn.
exercises.hint: classList.add('lit') turns it on. classList.remove('lit') turns it off.

checks.label: the 'lit' class was added to the square

exercises.title: Score display
tasks: Create a variable called score and set it to 0.
Set its textContent to show the score: 'Score: 0'.
Now update score to 10 and update the display to match.
Update score to 25 and update the display again.
exercises.hint: Use a template literal to build the display string: `Score: ${score}`

challenges.title: The element isn't changing — why?
challenges.description: This code should change the text of a paragraph but nothing happens on the page. Find the bug and fix it.
challenges.buggyCode:
let message = document.getElementById('massege');
message.textContent = 'Hello from JavaScript!';
challenges.starterCode:
let message = document.getElementById('massege');
message.textContent = 'Hello from JavaScript!';
challenges.hint: Look very carefully at the id string inside getElementById. Then look at the HTML.

checks.label: the message element's text was changed

challenges.explanation: The id passed to getElementById is 'massege' — a typo. The actual id in the HTML is 'message'. JavaScript can't find 'massege', so it returns null, and calling .textContent on null crashes silently. Always double-check id strings match exactly.

challenges.title: The score display is showing the wrong thing
challenges.description: The score display should show 'Score: 42' but it's showing 'Score: 0'. The score variable is correct. Find what's wrong.
challenges.hint: Look carefully at what's inside the ${}. Is it the variable or the number?
challenges.explanation: The template literal uses ${0} — the number 0 directly — instead of ${score} the variable. So it always shows 'Score: 0' no matter what score is set to. Fix: change ${0} to ${score}.

challenges.title: The square won't light up
challenges.description: This code should light up a square when the game starts but the square stays dark. There are two bugs. Find both.
challenges.hint: JavaScript and CSS are both case-sensitive. Check the id and the class name against what's in the HTML and stylesheet.
challenges.explanation: Two case-sensitivity bugs: getElementById('Square') should be getElementById('square') — the id in the HTML is lowercase. And classList.add('Lit') should be classList.add('lit') — the CSS class is lowercase. Both need to match exactly.
